title: Pilot 'reading a cancer headline' module (method proof; 1 worked example end-to-end)
context: Before authoring the three full courses, the project needs a proof that the editorial method works end-to-end: the guardrail rubric, the misconception-targeting method, the provenance/citation pipeline, and the 'not medical advice' framing all tested together in one short worked example. This pilot module proves the production pipeline (rubric → cited aggregate data → worked example → misconception callout → self-check) before the larger content investment begins. The module works with publicly available, aggregate, cited cancer statistics — no individual patient data. It is statistical-tier content (medium risk), reviewed by the stats reviewer.
objective: Author a short pilot module — 'reading a cancer headline' — that demonstrates the editorial method end-to-end: one worked example that takes a real cancer statistic headline (e.g., '5-year survival has doubled'), deconstructs the misconception it causes (lead-time bias / survival ≠ mortality), names the error explicitly, shows the correct interpretation using cited aggregate data with data vintage, and closes with a self-check. The module must implement all editorial guardrail rules from oncology-data-literacy-editorial-001 and serve as the test case for the citation-coverage CI scaffold.
acceptanceCriteria: Produces a self-contained worked-example module that takes one real cancer statistic headline, names the misconception it triggers, and corrects it using cited aggregate data with data vintage and provenance

Implements all rules from the editorial guardrail rubric (oncology-data-literacy-editorial-001): no uncited statistic, correct-endpoint rule, absolute-before-relative framing, no medical advice frame present, uncertainty shown
Every statistical assertion in the module is keyed to a register entry (or a temporary provenance stub) with data vintage; the citation-coverage scaffold passes on this module
Module includes a named misconception callout and a self-check item tied to the misconception inventory
Reviewed and signed off by the statistical (biostatistician/epidemiologist) reviewer; sign-off recorded in the reviewers ledger
output: A short, reviewed pilot module ('reading a cancer headline') that proves the editorial method and production pipeline end-to-end, with all statistics cited with data vintage, the misconception named and corrected, a self-check included, and stats-reviewer sign-off recorded.
